design_philosophy.core_loop: Navigate protagonist through obstacle courses while managing gate mechanics controlled by R.O.B. robot accessory or second controller
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Synchronization between player action and R.O.B.'s slow mechanical response time; coordination challenge between two controllers/devices
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Operating a sophisticated robotic companion as an interactive ally rather than playing a traditional video game
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Deliberately positioned as a toy/robotics experience rather than pure video game; designed for novelty and spectacle over pure gameplay depth. R.O.B. is explicitly a Trojan Horse marketing device to rebrand NES away from video game crash stigma. Game is fully playable without R.O.B. via Controller 2, revealing the accessory as optional gimmick.

difficulty.lives_system: Multiple lives per round; can lose lives through obstacle collision
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Can be played in multiple modes: with R.O.B. (slow, novelty-focused), or with Controller 2 (faster, more skill-focused). Players can adjust difficulty implicitly by choosing control method.

progression.unlock_mechanism: Stage progression through completing obstacle courses
replayability_hooks: Two distinct play modes (R.O.B. vs Controller 2) change pacing and skill requirements
Speedrunning incentive due to mechanical slowness when using R.O.B.

sources_used: The Cutting Room Floor - Gyromite article
Wikipedia - R.O.B. article (History and Development sections)
